questions.question: Which selector matches elements that have a specific attribute?
questions.explanation: [attr] selects all elements that have the specified attribute, regardless of its value.

questions.question: What does [href^='https'] match?
questions.explanation: The ^= operator matches attribute values that START with the specified string. [href^='https'] matches secure links.

questions.question: Which selector matches elements whose attribute ends with a specific value?
questions.explanation: $= is the ends-with operator. For example, [href$='.pdf'] matches all links to PDF files.

questions.question: How do you make an attribute selector case-insensitive?
options: Add i before the closing bracket
questions.explanation: Adding the i flag before the closing bracket makes the matching case-insensitive, e.g., [type='email' i].

questions.question: Which attribute selector would match a[href$='.pdf'] for a PDF link?
questions.explanation: $= matches the end of the attribute value. [href$='.pdf'] matches links whose href ends with '.pdf'.

questions.question: What does the [attr~='value'] selector do?
options: Matches if value is a whole word in a space-separated list
Matches if value contains the substring
Matches if value starts with the string
questions.explanation: The ~= operator matches if the attribute value contains the specified word as a whole word in a space-separated list.

questions.question: Which attribute selector matches elements with a lang attribute starting with 'en'?
questions.explanation: [lang|='en'] matches lang='en' exactly or lang starting with 'en-' (like en-US, en-GB). It's specifically designed for language subtags.

questions.question: What is the specificity of attribute selectors?
questions.explanation: Attribute selectors have the same specificity as classes and pseudo-classes: 0,1,0.

questions.question: How can you style all disabled form elements using attribute selectors?
questions.explanation: [disabled] selects any element with the disabled attribute. This is often combined with a tag selector: input[disabled].

questions.question: Which selector would match an input that is both required and of type email?
questions.explanation: Chaining attribute selectors (input[required][type='email']) matches elements that satisfy ALL conditions simultaneously.

questions.question: What does the [attr*='value'] selector match?
questions.explanation: *= matches if the attribute value contains the specified string anywhere within it. [class*='icon'] matches class='icon-large'.

questions.question: Which attribute selector would style external links differently from internal ones?
questions.explanation: Both a[href^='http'] and a[rel='external'] can target external links. Using the href pattern is automatic; using rel requires HTML markup.

questions.question: Can attribute selectors be combined with pseudo-classes?
questions.explanation: Attribute selectors can be combined with any pseudo-class, e.g., input[type='email']:focus { border-color: blue; }.

questions.question: How can you select elements with a data-attribute value of 'active' using attribute selectors?
questions.explanation: [data-status='active'] performs an exact match. Data attributes work perfectly with all attribute selector patterns.

questions.question: What is a performance consideration for attribute selectors?
options: They are faster than class selectors
They are slower than class selectors on very large DOMs
They only work in modern browsers
questions.explanation: Attribute selectors require checking each element's attributes and are slower than class selectors, especially on large DOMs.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a valid attribute selector?
questions.explanation: There is no != operator in CSS attribute selectors. Use :not([attr='value']) to exclude matching values.

questions.question: What does the selector a[href*='github.com'] do?
options: Matches links exactly equal to github.com
questions.explanation: *= matches any substring. a[href*='github.com'] matches https://github.com, http://www.github.com, and any URL containing github.com.

questions.question: How can you style elements with both class='card' and data-featured='true'?
questions.explanation: .card[data-featured='true'] combines a class selector with an attribute selector. Multiple selectors can be chained for precise targeting.

questions.question: What browser support do attribute selectors have?
questions.explanation: All standard attribute selectors have been supported since Internet Explorer 7. Only the case-insensitive i flag requires modern browsers.

questions.question: Which attribute selector is most appropriate for styling elements with aria-current='page'?
questions.explanation: Exact match [aria-current='page'] is the most precise and appropriate for targeting the current page indicator in navigation.
